nytimes_articles.headline: Corrections
New Jersey's Tax Cuts Have Worked
nytimes_articles.abstract:
States around the country are cutting taxes, and no state has received more attention than New Jersey, where Gov. Christine Todd Whitman's income tax cuts have made her a rising Republican star. But because all states pass most of their revenues back to local governments, critics say that states' cuts will force local property taxes to rise -- leaving taxpayers no better off. 

     A study we have done on New Jersey's taxes for the Manhattan Institute indicates that such criticism is unjustified. We found that in the majority of towns and cities, property taxes have not risen since Governor Whitman's cuts took effect. Even in the municipalities that have raised taxes, property owners  are still saving money and will continue to do so.

A Performer Is Not a Teacher
nytimes_articles.abstract:
After years of desperation and neglect, the arts education community in New York is ecstatic. The Annenberg Foundation has announced a grant of $12 million, which with matching funds would bring a $36 million, five-year arts program into the New York City public schools. But the plan that a consulting group, Artsvision, has developed for the grant money is misguided and should be reconsidered. 

     To make a real difference in children's lives, a public school arts program must provide regular, sequential training by skilled and experienced teachers. Artsvision acknowledges the importance of instruction but is willing to leave the choice of instructors, and what they will teach, to outside groups: "Individual schools, or groups of schools will form partnerships with arts organizations, community-based organizations, artists or colleges," it says. Classes would be run by outside performers and artists, untrained classroom teachers or parents -- not by trained arts teachers.

The People's Holocaust
nytimes_articles.abstract:
One day in November 1942, at the height of the Germans' systematic slaughter  of Polish Jewry, men serving in a battalion of about 500 reserve policemen learned that they would be undertaking yet another genocidal killing, this time of the Jews of Lukow. 

     That evening, according to one of the battalion members, a peculiar thing happened: "An entertainment unit of Berlin policemen . . . were our guests. . .  . The members of this unit had also learned of the forthcoming shooting of the Jews and indeed offered, even pleaded emphatically, for permission to participate in the execution of these Jews. This strange request was granted by  the battalion."

nytimes_articles.headline: EUROPE '96: FESTIVALS;Music in Grand Irish Houses
nytimes_articles.abstract:
EVERY June for the last 26 years, there has been a festival of music in great Irish houses, and although the concerts are open to the public, getting the word out has not been a top priority. We found out about it quite by accident -- eavesdropping in a restaurant. My husband, Wil, and I go to Ireland every couple of years, and the combination of music, architecture and gardens struck us as an opportunity to indulge our interest in all three. Aside from these attractions, there is the Irish countryside. Getting from A to B (never as the crow flies and occasionally stopped dead by a cattle fair in the middle of the village) is half the fun. The festival takes place over a period of two weeks, and consists of a series of concerts in grand houses and historic buildings. The venues vary from year to year -- a couple of the concerts are always in Dublin,  the rest scattered about the country. 

     We started proceedings in March with a phone call to the Irish Tourist Board, which produced a list of the concerts and venues "subject to amendment." There were 11 in all, 3 of them repeats (both musicians and programs), and 3 in places that we'd already seen. To coordinate concerts we might attend with places where we might stay we studied a map and an invaluable brochure called "The Hidden Ireland," which lists some 40 private country houses of character, comfort and good food that take paying guests.

nytimes_articles.headline: The People's Holocaust
nytimes_articles.abstract:
One day in November 1942, at the height of the Germans' systematic slaughter  of Polish Jewry, men serving in a battalion of about 500 reserve policemen learned that they would be undertaking yet another genocidal killing, this time of the Jews of Lukow. 

     That evening, according to one of the battalion members, a peculiar thing happened: "An entertainment unit of Berlin policemen . . . were our guests. . .  . The members of this unit had also learned of the forthcoming shooting of the Jews and indeed offered, even pleaded emphatically, for permission to participate in the execution of these Jews. This strange request was granted by  the battalion."

nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE roomful of hair stylists, manicurists and salon owners was engrossed as a slide projection showed a rushing river. "Write this down," Erica Miller ordered the participants in the session she was leading at last week's 79th International Beauty Show in Manhattan. "Water will revitalize you." 

     The massage therapist turned esthetician (the kind that worries about your skin) turned author urged audience members to transform their salons into "day spas," or risk being "antiquated by the year 2000."

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: MANHATTAN BEACH/SHEEPSHEAD BAY;Beach Path Ends in Legal Bills
nytimes_articles.abstract:
It has been a bad few years for those who live on Manhattan Beach. 

     Their 120-year-old strolling path on the ocean, known as the Esplanade, buckled from ceaseless battery by the waves and a few voracious storms. Then a neighbor, Jack Laboz, erected a fence that barred anyone from walking along the  shore. And last month, after a local community group spent at least $50,000 to prove that public access to the shore was a historical community right, they lost.

Jazz Trombonist: Stylist and Composer
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Ray Anderson, a jazz trombonist, is not an easy man to reach -- at least not  at his Long Island home. 

     "Can we postpone this?" he pleaded to an expected caller one morning. He had  recently returned from a week's residency at a performing arts high school in Philadelphia, been up all night finishing a commission for a European youth orchestra, which he will be leading in Copenhagen next summer, and was leaving the next day for a month in Europe with one of his combos.

Obsession
nytimes_articles.abstract: CHEKHOV'S PLAYS An Opening Into Eternity. By Richard Gilman. 261 pp. New Haven: Yale University Press. $30.    RICHARD GILMAN'S book, as he acknowledges in his introduction, is rooted in an obsession. Traveling around Russia in the glasnost year 1989, he was "continually remembering Chekhov stories and plays, inspired by an incident, a face, a venue . . . or even by an object such as a samovar, which always makes me think of 'Uncle Vanya,' or an old clock, which brings to mind 'Three Sisters.' " Even upon his arrival at Sheremetevo Airport in Moscow -- a most unromantic place -- he saw some straggly birches and remembered Chekhov's phrase "dear, modest birches." But above all it was at Yalta, where the doctor-playwright spent the last years of his short life, that the visiting American felt happily overwhelmed. The annual meeting of the Chekhov Society was taking place; he was impressed by the sense he had of all being on intimate terms with their hero. Invited unexpectedly to speak, he gave a "rambling and disjointed" talk, which received "a generous round of applause." Following an interview on Crimean television, he saw his image in a newspaper, "bearded, gesturing, a Chekhov fanatic."

UP AND COMING: Kcho;Art Is a Cuban's Vessel to Freedom
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHEN HE FIRST ARRIVED in New York from Havana in January, Kcho (whose original name was Alexis Leyva Machado) met a man who had emigrated from Cuba years before. "How did you get here?" the man asked. "Were you held in Guantanamo?" 

     The 26-year-old sculptor, whose professional name derives from, and is pronounced like, his nickname, Cacho, explained that he was in New York on a three-month visa to prepare for his first show in this country at the Barbara Gladstone Gallery on Greene Street in SoHo. The man was amazed. "You came by airplane, not raft? Que rico!"

Historic Tours Focus on Women
nytimes_articles.abstract:
MAIDEN nieces dependent on a distinguished uncle, ladies overseeing household servants on a 19th-century estate, young women playing the pianoforte: those could be the elements of a new Jane Austen film, but the scene is American, aspects of daily life at historic Sunnyside here. 

     The emphasis is on women during tours at Sunnyside and at Philipsburg Manor in North Tarrytown during March, which is Women's History Month. Then, starting  in April, general tours resume at those sites and at Van Cortlandt Manor in Croton-on-Hudson and Montgomery Place in Annandale-on-Hudson, all operated by Historic Hudson Valley. But new information about the daily lives of women has also been incorporated into the regular tours at the four properties.

The Birth of a Theatrical Comet
BOOKEND;Bernard Malamud: Behind the Poker Face
nytimes_articles.abstract:
To watch Bernard Malamud play poker was to watch him write. For years we shared a poker game in Bennington, Vt. He divided his time between city and country -- spending winters in Manhattan, then returning to New England after the worst storms had passed. In the game there were faculty members from Bennington College, musicians, painters, sculptors; there was the local millionaire and the man who ran the gas station; we took turns playing host. Malamud would always call well in advance to plan for his turn; he'd say, always, "Count me in." 

     He was serious rather than sportive; poker relaxed him, he said. But you couldn't tell that, really, from the way he played the game or how hard he studied the hand he was dealt and how he bet, bluffed, pondered, folded, raised. He came formally dressed to the table, arriving on the hour and departing when he'd said he would; well organized and solemn on those Thursday nights, he pursed his lips in concentration and adjusted his eyeglasses often and joked and chattered sparingly and allowed himself only one beer. He kept his cards close to his chest.

Silence Spoken Here
nytimes_articles.abstract:
FORGET about mastering a foreign language. We're too old, it's too hard and life is too short. And perhaps, after all, silence is the best language for travelers. 

      I didn't always feel this way. For a long time, I let my lack of fluency in  foreign languages hold me back. I felt awkward, stupid and ashamed that we Americans aren't taught a foreign language from the time we're small. I still cringe at the cultural imperialism that has us traipsing off to Europe, Asia and Africa, expecting that all the world speak English.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: MANHATTAN BEACH/SHEEPSHEAD BAY;Fear Changes Cabbies' Habits
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Last month, a livery cabdriver was killed by a gunshot to the back of his head on a quiet street in Sheepshead Bay. It was a day after another driver was  slain less than a mile away, his body found hung over the steering wheel and his money gone. 

     The police say these murders, and a third one in Sheepshead Bay last year, were probably committed by the same killer because the same .22-caliber pistol was used. Eugene Pero, president of the Livery Drivers Coalition, said four livery drivers had been killed in the city this year, 37 last year and 52 in 1994.

SOAPBOX;Calling Any Cop Named Patrick
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE annual St. Patrick's Day Parade along Kennedy Boulevard is such a tradition in Jersey City -- with its legions of bagpipers, shamrock-wearing priests, antique cars, Miss Colleen contestants and much else -- that you might  suppose it had gone on forever. In fact, it dates back just three decades, and its beginnings were not what you might call respectable. 

     It was born in the Bergen Bar on the eve of St. Patrick's Day 1962. I should  know; I was there. Five of us -- sad sons of the Ould Sod -- were drowning our sorrows, lamenting that Jersey City, unlike neighboring Newark and New York, did not have a big parade to honor Ireland's patron saint.

     Like almost any marina, the basin looks romantic. Like many things that look  romantic, it's anything but.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: MANHATTAN BEACH/SHEEPSHEAD BAY;Lundy's Leads a Street's Revival
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The restaurant is one of glee-evoking legend, where families devoured pink chowders, nibbled on light biscuits and dined on lobster. And now when people enter the new Lundy's, they saunter across the clay-colored floor and often smile nostalgically as they witness the shucking at the clam bar, the soft lights on the golden pillars and the waiters resting large plates of food on the white-clothed tables. 

     "I'm hoping they still have the french fried onion rings," said Margarita Garcia, as she scanned the multilevel restaurant from the hostesses' desk during a visit with her husband. "It is quieter."

ATLANTIC CITY;Irish in Name Only
VOICES: VIEWPOINT;Some Postcard Postscripts
nytimes_articles.abstract:
SUPPORTERS of the flat tax claim that by doing away with scores of deductions, exemptions and credits, the flat tax will create a loophole-free, airtight system that is so simple even large corporations will report their taxes on postcard-size returns. In reality, the flat tax -- as detailed in the legislation introduced by Representative Dick Armey, the House majority leader -- will create gaping loopholes that cannot be closed without a new set of complicated rules. 

     These loopholes arise because the flat tax treats different types of income differently -- the same engine that is behind most of today's tax-avoidance strategies. The Armey flat tax imposes a 17 percent tax on sales and services income but does not tax financial income, like dividends, interest and capital gains. Financial income is exempt under the flax tax because business income is  supposed to be taxed only once.

Park Officials Take Steps to Save Rainbow Trout From Disease
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IN an effort to prevent Long Island waters from becoming infected with a disease that is deadly to rainbow trout, officials of the Connetquot River State Park have announced new regulations for fly fishermen who will begin the trout season in the park's waters on April 1. 

     The affliction, known as whirling disease, has already devastated the wild rainbow trout population in many of the finest trout rivers in Montana and other western states, and it has recently been detected in some upstate New York waterways.  Long Island has remained unaffected, but highly vulnerable, because  of the popularity of rainbows among local anglers.

nytimes_articles.headline: STYLE;Green Alert
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Consider green, a color more important than you think. There is the green associated with plaid pants on the golf course, British racing cars, guacamole,  being sick before throwing up, dental floss and envy. Green is the only color that gets it own day. Some political analysts believe the Red Menace of Communism has been replaced by the Green Menace of Islamic fundamentalism. When  I learned that green -- precisely, acid green, was au courant this fashion season, I asked people if they had any green opinions. 

     Roz Chast, cartoonist: "Green is sort of an honorary fourth primary color. When you look at orange, it is obviously red and yellow. Purple is obviously red and blue. But I don't look at green and think it's yellow-blue. Also, green is scary. If you wrote the word 'radiation' you would have to write in in green."

wiki_events.headline: A nightclub fire in Quezon City, Philippines kills 162 people.

pages.title: Ozone Disco fire
pages.description: 1996 nightclub fire in Quezon City, Philippines
pages.abstract: The Ozone Disco fire in Quezon City, Philippines, broke out on March 18, 1996, leaving at least 162 people dead. It is officially acknowledged as the worst fire in Philippine history, and among the 10 worst nightclub fires in the world.

pages.title: Quezon City
pages.description: Highly urbanized city in Metro Manila, Philippines
pages.abstract: Quezon City, also known as the City of Quezon and Q.C., is the most populous city in the Philippines. According to the 2024 census, it has a population of 3,084,270 people. It was founded on October 12, 1939, and was named after Manuel L. Quezon, the second president of the Philippines. Quezon City served as the capital of the Philippines from 1948 until 1976, when the designation was returned to Manila.
